1: kaved (kābēd, H3513): 'I have made heavy / hardened his heart.' The plague narrative employs three distinct Hebrew verbs for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart, each with a different shade of meaning: kaved (to be heavy, weighty — used of Pharaoh hardening his own heart in earlier plagues), chazaq (to be strong, firm — used most often when YHWH is the subject), and qashah (to be stiff, stubborn — rare, more extreme). The theological complexity is intentional: Pharaoh's hardness is simultaneously self-willed and divinely purposed. The purpose clause lemaan siti ototi eleh beqirbo ('that I may place these my signs in his midst') frames the entire plague cycle as a divine self-disclosure to Egypt, Israel, and all nations. Paul cites Exodus 9:16 and Exodus 4's pattern in Romans 9:17-18 to ground the doctrine of divine sovereignty in election.
2: ulemaan tesapper beoznei vincha u-ven bincha: 'so that you may tell in the ears of your son and your son's son.' The generational transmission of the Exodus memory is a structural motif in the Torah: the plagues are not only acts of judgment but acts of testimony, performed so that the story can be told. Sippur (telling) is the root of the Passover Seder's Haggadah (telling/narrating). The phrase hit'alalti (I have dealt harshly, or I have made sport) uses a Hithpael reflexive form — YHWH does to Egypt what Egypt did to Israel. The table-turning theme is fundamental to the Exodus narrative's justice logic.
3: anah lehathnot lifnai: 'refuse to humble yourself before me.' The Hiphil of ana (to humble, afflict) asks whether Pharaoh will submit to YHWH's sovereign authority. The same root (ana, H6031) is used for the affliction Israel suffered in Egypt (Exodus 1:11-12) — YHWH now reverses the dynamic: the one who afflicted Israel is challenged to humble himself before YHWH. The refusal to humble oneself (anah) is the root sin of the Exodus: Pharaoh's ge'ut (pride) against YHWH's kavod (glory). James 4:10 and 1 Peter 5:6 ('Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand') draw on this vocabulary.
4: arbeh (ʾarbeh, H697): locusts — from the root rava (to multiply, be numerous). The name itself evokes the swarm's characteristic: countless multitudes. Locusts in the ANE were the most feared agricultural catastrophe — they could strip a region of all vegetation in hours. Egyptian reliefs and texts document locust plagues as national disasters. Joel 1-2 uses the locust army as the primary image for YHWH's eschatological army of judgment. The eighth plague is the longest and most verbally elaborate of the plague accounts, with extensive dialogue between Moses and Pharaoh's officials.
5: vichisa et-ein ha'aretz: 'they will cover the eye of the land' — a vivid idiom: ein (eye) here means the visible surface, the appearance. When the locusts cover the earth, the land's face cannot be seen. The same idiom appears in Numbers 22:5, 11, where the Israelites 'cover the eye of the land.' The completeness of the plague is emphasized: even what the hail (barad, plague 7) left behind will be devoured. The cumulative destruction of seven plagues on Egypt's agriculture is now being completed.
6: umale'u vatecha u-vatei kol-avadecha: 'they will fill your palace and the houses of all your officials.' The locust invasion invades even the private dwellings — the inner sanctum of Egyptian society is not protected. Melo (fullness, filling) echoes YHWH's claim in Psalms 24:1: 'The earth is YHWH's and its fullness (umelo'ah)' — the locusts, as YHWH's instrument, fill what YHWH owns. The emphasis on something 'your fathers and their fathers never saw since the day they came to this ground' establishes the unprecedented severity: the plagues exceed all historical memory.
7: hayehi ha'ish moked lanu: 'Will this man be a snare/trap for us?' The noun moked (H4170) means a trap or snare, from the root meaning to ensnare. Pharaoh's own officials recognize the untenable situation: Egypt is being ruined (avad, perishing) for the sake of Pharaoh's stubbornness. The officials' intervention is the first crack in the Egyptian royal court's solidarity with Pharaoh. Their use of 'do you not yet know that Egypt is destroyed?' (terem teda ki avdah mitzrayim) employs a rhetorical question that functions as statement: Egypt is already ruined.
8: mi vaʾmi haholchim: 'who and who are going?' — Pharaoh's interrogative is a negotiating tactic to limit which Israelites will worship. He will allow the men but not the women, children, or property. The exchange exposes the false nature of his offer: the worship of YHWH ('we will hold a festival to YHWH') requires the whole community — uvizqenenu uvina'arenu bvanenu uvivnotenu uvetzoneinu uvivqarenu (with our old and young, sons and daughters, flocks and herds). The pattern of partial concessions (Exodus 8, 10) followed by retraction is a literary device showing the progressive hardening.
9: bina'arenu u-vizqenenu nelech: 'with our young and old we will go.' Moses's response brooks no compromise: the entire community — every generation and all livestock — must participate in YHWH's festival. The totality requirement is theological: YHWH's worship is not a partial or partial-community activity. This prefigures the NT understanding that the gospel summons whole households (Acts 16:31-34), and that there is 'neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female' in Christ (Galatians 3:28) — all are included.
10: yihyeh YHWH imachem: 'May YHWH be with you — if I let you and your little ones go!' Pharaoh's sarcastic blessing becomes ironic: YHWH will indeed be with them, precisely by overcoming Pharaoh's opposition. Ra lifneichem ('evil/harm is before you') — Pharaoh accuses Moses of having evil intentions. The word ra (evil, harm) is the same word used in the Tree of Knowledge of good and evil (tov vara) — Pharaoh positions himself as the arbiter of good (staying in Egypt) and evil (going to worship).
11: vayegaresh otam meim pnei pharoh: 'and they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence' — the Piel of garash (to drive out, expel) is used: a forcible expulsion from Pharaoh's court. Moses and Aaron are expelled — the same verb used of expulsion from the garden in Genesis 3:24 (yigaresh) and used of Israel's later expulsion from Canaan by its enemies. The expulsion from Pharaoh's presence is a bitter irony: Pharaoh drives out the only mediators who could end the plague.
12: neteh yadcha al-eretz mitzrayim: 'stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt.' Moses's outstretched staff/hand is the recurring sign-gesture of YHWH's power acting through the human agent. Eight of the ten plagues involve Moses stretching out his staff or hand (exceptions: plagues 1, 5, 6). The gesture embodies the covenant relationship: YHWH acts through human agency, not apart from it. The raised hand of Moses in battle against Amalek (Exodus 17:11) follows the same pattern — human gesture as conduit of divine power.
13: ruach qadim (east wind): the desert wind blowing from the east (Sinai, Arabia) — hot, dry, powerful, and in the ANE often understood as an ominous wind of divine judgment (cf. Hosea 13:15: 'an east wind from YHWH will come'). The east wind drove the locusts overnight; when YHWH reversed it to a ruach yam (sea/west wind, Exodus 10:19), the locusts were swept into the Red Sea. YHWH controls both the natural forces and their direction — the plague and its removal are both divine acts.
14: lifanav lo hayah khen arbeh: 'before him there was no such locust swarm' — the superlative formula (before never, after never) applied to the eighth plague echoes the same formula in Joel 2:2 ('there has been nothing like it before, nor will be again after it'). Joel's eschatological locust army uses the Exodus eighth plague as its type. The quantity description — kaved meod (very heavy, very severe) — links the plague severity to the vocabulary of the hardening (kaved): both Pharaoh's heart and the plague are described by the same root, as if the natural disaster bears the imprint of the spiritual condition that caused it.
15: vayechashach ha'aretz: 'the land became dark' — the Hiphil of chashach (to be/become dark) is used; the locusts blackened the visible surface of the land. Lo nivkah kol-yerek be'etz u-veesev hasadeh ('nothing green remained on the trees or among the grasses of the field') — total agricultural devastation. Egypt's economy was agricultural; the Nile's annual flooding made Egypt the ancient world's breadbasket. Seven plagues targeting crops, livestock, and water systematically dismantled Egypt's food security.
16: chataʾti l'YHWH eloheichem v'lachem: 'I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you.' The first explicit verbal confession of sin by Pharaoh — using the standard term chata (to sin, miss the mark, H2398). The dual object (against YHWH and against you) acknowledges both the vertical and horizontal dimensions of wrong. Pharaoh makes similar confessions in Exodus 9:27-28 and Exodus 10:16, but each time reverts after the plague is removed. The confessions without genuine repentance are a biblical illustration of the difference between remorse and repentance (metanoia) — the same distinction Paul draws in 2 Corinthians 7:10: 'worldly grief produces death.'
17: sa na chataʾti: 'please forgive my sin just this once' — the Qal imperative of nasa (to lift up, carry, forgive) with na (please, particle of entreaty). The idiom nasa chet (lift/carry sin) is the standard Hebrew for forgiveness; YHWH is the one who “lifts up” sin (Exodus 34:7; Numbers 14:18; Psalms 32:5). Pharaoh's request uses exactly the right theological vocabulary — but the subsequent verse shows the hardened heart persisting after the plague's removal. Prayer for relief during judgment without transformation afterward is one of the plague narrative's defining themes.
18: vayetze meim pharoh vayitpallel el-YHWH: 'he went out from Pharaoh and prayed to YHWH.' Moses's intercession for Pharaoh — despite Pharaoh's persistent wickedness — is a remarkable act of prophetic intercession. Moses functions here as a type of the Christological intercessor: praying for oppressors who continue to resist (Luke 23:34: 'Father, forgive them'). The verb palal (Hithpael: to pray, intercede) becomes the standard term for intercessory prayer throughout the OT.
19: ruach yam chazaq meod: 'a very strong west wind' (ruach yam, literally sea-wind). YHWH reverses the wind direction to remove the locusts: what he sent by east wind he removes by west wind. The locusts are cast into the yam suf (Red Sea/Sea of Reeds) — the same body of water Israel will cross and Egypt will drown in. The sea that drowns the locusts will drown Pharaoh's army. YHWH's complete control over the natural world is the implicit theology: storm, wind, sea — all serve as instruments of his judgment and deliverance.
20: vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev paroh: 'YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart' — the Piel of chazaq (to strengthen, make firm, harden). This is the most common verb used when YHWH is the agent of the hardening. The three-verb hardening vocabulary of the plague narrative (kaved, chazaq, qashah) represents a progression: Pharaoh hardened his own heart first (Exodus 7-9); YHWH then confirms and strengthens that hardening as judgment. Paul's use of Exodus 9:16 in Romans 9:17 grounds the doctrine that divine sovereignty operates through human choices, not apart from them — God's hardening of Pharaoh is the judicial confirmation of Pharaoh's own self-hardening.
21: veyamesh choshech: 'a darkness that may be felt/groped' — a hapax phrase using mamash (to touch, feel). The darkness of the ninth plague is physically tangible: not merely absence of light but a positive, material quality. Exodus Rabbah connects this to the primordial tohu (formlessness) of Genesis 1:2: 'the earth was formless and dark (choshech).' YHWH's darkness-plague reverses creation's order — three days of uncreation precede the death of Egypt's firstborn. The number three echoes the three days of creation's light-establishment (Genesis 1:3-13). The darkness-to-light pattern is the foundation of Paul's new creation language: 'God who said “let light shine out of darkness” has shone in our hearts' (2 Corinthians 4:6).
22: vayehiy choshech-afelah bechol-eretz mitzrayim shloshet yamim: 'there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.' The double term choshech afelah (darkness of thick/pitchy darkness) intensifies beyond ordinary darkness into absolute deprivation of light. Egyptians worshiped Ra the sun-god as their supreme deity — three days of total darkness were a direct assault on the supreme Egyptian god. The ninth plague is among the most theologically pointed: YHWH claims dominion over the very light that Egypt considered divine. The parallel darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45: 'darkness over all the land from the sixth to the ninth hour') deliberately evokes this plague — judgment-darkness at the moment of the new covenant Passover.
23: ulekhol benei Yisrael hayah or bimoshvotam: 'but all the Israelites had light in their dwelling places.' The light/darkness contrast between Israel and Egypt is a pattern throughout the plagues: YHWH makes a distinction (hafla) between his people and Egypt. The dwelling places (moshvotam) of Israel are illuminated while Egypt gropes. This pattern appears in the Passover night itself (Exodus 12:23: the destroyer does not enter Israel's blood-marked homes) and ultimately in Revelation's new Jerusalem: 'the city has no need of sun or moon, for the glory of God gives it light' (Revelation 21:23).
24: rak tzoneichem u-vaqarchem yutztav: 'only your flocks and herds shall be left behind.' Pharaoh's fourth concession offer: go with your families, but leave your animals. Moses refuses because the animals are needed for the sacrifices — ki mimenu nikach la'avod et-YHWH eloheinu ('from them we will take to serve YHWH our God'). The animals belong to the worship of YHWH; to leave them in Egypt is to keep the means of sacrifice captive. Moses's insistence that lo tippalter parsa ('not a hoof shall be left') is a declaration that YHWH's worship requires total mobilization of resources — nothing can remain in bondage.
25: gam atah titen beyadenu zevachim ve'olot: 'you yourself must provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings to present to YHWH our God.' Moses turns the tables: not only will Israel take their own livestock, but Pharaoh must contribute to the sacrifices. The demand illustrates the comprehensive reversal: Egypt's wealth will fund Israel's worship of YHWH. This prefigures the spoiling of Egypt (Exodus 12:35-36) and the NT pattern of earthly resources being redirected to the service of God's kingdom.
26: vegam miqnenenu yelech imanu lo tishar parsa: 'our livestock will also go with us; not a hoof will be left.' The idiom lo tishar parsa (not a hoof shall remain) became a proverbial expression for complete exit/departure. The explanation given — we don't know what YHWH will require until we arrive — is theologically important: worship cannot be planned in advance from a posture of compromise with slavery. Full exodus precedes authentic worship. This anti-negotiation principle runs through the NT: the gospel does not accommodate gradual partial repentance; it calls for complete reorientation.
27: vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev paroh: The tenth hardening reference in the plague narrative. The alternation between self-hardening (Pharaoh's own choice) and divine hardening (YHWH confirming the pattern) is now nearing its climax: the next plague will be decisive. The repeated hardening-and-reprieve cycle illustrates what Hebrews warns against: the danger of continued exposure to divine truth producing not softening but increased hardness (Hebrews 3:7-8: 'Today if you hear his voice do not harden your hearts'; Exodus 3:13: 'hardened through the deceitfulness of sin').
28: hishamer lecha al-tosef reot panai: 'guard yourself, do not see my face again.' The Niphal reflexive/passive of shamar (to guard, keep) used reflexively: 'guard yourself.' Pharaoh's threat carries irony: he is the one who should guard himself against YHWH. The prohibition of seeing Moses's face mirrors the earlier encounter where Moses could not see YHWH's face (Exodus 33:20) — but the roles are reversed: a mortal potentate threatens the prophet of YHWH. Pharaoh's final rejection of Moses will be met by the final plague — the death of the firstborn.
29: ken dibarta: 'you have spoken correctly/rightly.' Moses affirms Pharaoh's statement — he will indeed never see Pharaoh's face again. The Hebrew ken (thus, rightly) is emphatic. Moses's departure is a prophetic symbolic act: the prophet's withdrawal from the king signals the withdrawal of divine grace and the inauguration of the final judgment. This scene closes the plague-cycle's dialogue phase; the tenth plague will require no further negotiation.
